articles.headline: Address delivered before the Colored Female Dorcas Society of Buffalo (concluded)
articles.summary: Concluding half of a benevolence/charity address delivered before the Colored Female Dorcas Society of Buffalo, published by request of the Society; began in the Feb. 11, 1837 issue. The byline names the speaker as 'a colored man' with a surname OCR renders inconsistently as 'Rosear/Rosca Basus/Basks' (uncertain reading -- not matched to a specific person). He praises the Society's growth and finances, urges young men of Buffalo to form benevolent/literary societies on the model of Philadelphia's ~60 beneficent societies, and cites Philadelphia's Black community spending $9,600 annually supporting its own poor beyond a $2,500 poor tax.

articles.headline: Circular Letter to the Free Colored Citizens of the United States
articles.summary: Circular letter from New York City Black clergy endorsing four agents appointed by the American Anti-Slavery Society to labor among the free people of color (education, temperance, and church support); the agents' names are badly OCR-garbled and not confidently identified. Signed by Christopher Rush (Superintendent, Zion Church, Methodist Connexion), Isaac Barney (Elder, African Union Methodist Church), an elder of Asbury Methodist Church/Zion's Connexion whose first name OCRs as 'Timothy Etoe', James Simmons (Elder, Zion's Methodist Church), John T. Raymond (Elder, Zion's Baptist Church), R. S. Wright (Pastor, Presbyterian Church, Frankford Street), and Samuel E. Cornish (Presbyterian). Also includes a letter from agent Wm. S. Jennings (Boston) describing the Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society's anniversary meeting being forced into a stable loft after Faneuil Hall was denied them.

articles.headline: Phoenixonian Literary Society semi-annual meeting
articles.summary: Report of the Phoenixonian (Phoenix) Literary Society's semi-annual meeting, evening of Thursday Feb. 9, 1837, New York City. Mr. B. Reason, President, stated the meeting's objects. Program included a poem 'Alonzo' composed and recited by Thomas S. Sidney; an address on 'The Influence of Intellectual Ability' by Thomas S. Sidney; an address (subject partly illegible, praised for research and imagination) by Charles Reason; an address read in absentia for Mr. Garnett (a member currently pursuing studies at the Oneida Institute); a partial address read by Mr. Zuill/Zuille, cut short by the lateness of the hour; and a closing colloquy on the times. The paper's editors call it one of the best evenings of entertainment they had attended in a long time.

articles.headline: Important Meeting of the People of Color in the City of New York (EXTRA)
articles.summary: Extra edition reporting a large meeting of New York City's Black community at Phenix Hall, Chapel Street, Monday evening Feb. 20, 1837. Henry Davis was called to the chair; Philip A. Bell and Edward V. Clark were appointed secretaries. Three petitions to the NY State Legislature were adopted and opened for signature: (1) repeal of the law authorizing the holding of a person as a slave in New York State; (2) a right to trial by jury for persons of color arrested as fugitive slaves; (3) extension of the right of suffrage to Black male citizens on the same terms as white male citizens. Full petition texts are printed, arguing from natural-rights and constitutional grounds. Notes a companion petition from Troy, NY signed by 39 men of color on suffrage, and a Society of Friends petition for trial-by-jury protections. Meeting adjourned to reconvene Wednesday, March 1, at the same hall.

articles.headline: Report and committee appointments, Phenix Hall meeting of Feb. 20, 1837
articles.summary: Follow-up report on the Feb. 20, 1837 Phenix Hall meeting (see the Feb. 22 Extra): confirms Henry Davis as chairman and Philip A. Bell / Edward V. Clark as secretaries, and records that Messrs. P. A. Bell, Henry Davis, and Thomas Van Rensselaer were nominated for the committee to carry the petitions to the Legislature at Albany. A five-person committee was also appointed to attend Phenix Hall daily to collect further signatures.

articles.headline: Interesting Letter -- discrimination at the North American Hotel, Lancaster, PA
articles.author: John C. Bowers
articles.summary: Letter dated Philadelphia, Feb. 22, 1837, from John C. Bowers to editor P. A. Bell. Bowers describes attending the Pennsylvania State Anti-Slavery Society's founding convention at Harrisburg, then, on the return trip, being ordered by the bar-keeper and landlord of the North American Hotel in Lancaster to leave the breakfast table because he was seated among white delegates. Bowers refused to move until he finished eating; the white delegates present, after a vote of the room (with a Mr. Gould posing the question), backed him and then withdrew from the table themselves in protest when the landlord insisted Bowers leave -- which Bowers presents as proof the abolitionists' principles were sincere, not merely rhetorical.

articles.headline: Paper renamed 'The Colored American'; Rev. Samuel E. Cornish named Editor
articles.author: Philip A. Bell
articles.summary: Landmark editorial by proprietor Philip A. Bell announcing that the Rev. Samuel E. Cornish -- founder of Freedom's Journal, the first newspaper published by and for Black Americans -- has accepted an invitation to take charge of the editorial department, and that the paper will thereafter be titled 'The Colored American' rather than 'The Weekly Advocate,' effective with the change taking hold under Cornish's editorial supervision. Bell states he will devote his full efforts to circulation and business management under the new arrangement with printer Robert Sears.

articles.headline: First issue as 'The Colored American' -- masthead and editorial transition
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Vol. I, No. 9 (March 4, 1837) is the first issue published under the new masthead 'The Colored American,' with Samuel E. Cornish as Editor and Philip A. Bell as Proprietor, motto 'Righteousness Exalteth a Nation.' Reprints Andrew Jackson's 1814 War of 1812 proclamations recruiting free Black Louisianans into militia service, and begins a series of extracts from the 1821 New York Constitutional Convention debates (Peter A. Jay, Chancellor Kent, Robert Clarke, Abraham Van Vechten) opposing the disfranchisement of Black voters.

articles.headline: Title of This Journal / Why We Should Have a Paper
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Editor Samuel E. Cornish's programmatic essay explaining the choice of 'Colored American' as the paper's name -- arguing Black Americans are more truly 'American' by birth and labor than many white citizens, rejecting the terms 'Negro,' 'African,' and 'black' as reproachful, and defending the qualifier 'colored' as necessary given the community's distinct circumstances. Lays out the paper's mission as the organ of colored Americans nationally, to unite scattered communities, oppose colonization and slavery, and fund lecturing agents from subscription surplus.

articles.headline: Death of Mrs. Henrietta Ray, wife of Rev. Charles B. Ray
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Extract from 'the Editor's Sermon' on the death of Mrs. Henrietta Ray, wife of the Rev. Charles B. Ray, who died in October 1836 aged 22 years, 3 months, 29 days, after a 16-month illness. Cornish recounts her early conversion, her insistence on adult baptism by immersion against advice, her founding presidency of a young ladies' literary and scientific society (alongside 'the lamented Abby Matthews' as a leading light of the Female Literary Society), and her patient death in the faith. Dated New York, Nov. 13, 1836, and printed in the March 4, 1837 issue.

articles.headline: Geneva Colored Anti-Slavery Society constitution and resolutions
articles.author: T. W. Derry (correspondent)
articles.summary: Constitution and resolutions of the 'Geneva Colored Anti-Slavery Society' (Geneva, N.Y.), formed Feb. 17, 1836 as an auxiliary to the New York State Anti-Slavery Society, forwarded to the paper by correspondent T. W. Derry of Geneva. H. Hawkins served as President and James W. Dulin (also rendered 'Duflin') as Secretary. Five resolutions adopted: the cause of abolition merits sacrifice and prayer; a concert of prayer for worldwide abolition; that unchristian treatment at the communion table is a sin to be abolished; that slaveholding ministers who preach against their own practice bring reproach on religion; and that the Society will support the Press as a means of moral suasion.

articles.headline: American Society for the Promotion of Education in Africa meets at Broadway Tabernacle
articles.summary: Report that the 'American Society for the Promotion of Education in Africa' (formerly the Young Men's Colonization Auxiliary Society of New-York) met at the Broadway Tabernacle on Wednesday evening Feb. 15, 1837, and adjourned to Feb. 21. The president cited the Society's break from colonization-movement partisanship as a reason for reorganizing under the new name and mission -- welcomed by editor Cornish as evidence of the old colonization society repenting its error.

events.name: Phenix Hall mass meeting for suffrage, trial by jury, and abolition petitions (New York City)
events.location: Phenix Hall, Chapel Street, New York City
events.description: Large meeting of New York City's free Black community, Monday evening Feb. 20, 1837, to adopt and circulate three petitions to the NY State Legislature: repeal of the state law authorizing slaveholding, a right to trial by jury for persons of color arrested as fugitive slaves, and extension of suffrage to Black men on equal terms with whites. Reported in the Feb. 22 Extra and recapped Feb. 25; a committee (P. A. Bell, Henry Davis, Thomas Van Rensselaer among those nominated) was to carry the petitions to Albany.
attendees: secretary of the meeting; nominated to Albany committee

events.name: Discrimination against John C. Bowers at the North American Hotel, Lancaster, PA
events.description: Returning from the Pennsylvania State Anti-Slavery Society's founding convention at Harrisburg, John C. Bowers was ordered to leave the breakfast table at the North American Hotel because he was seated among white anti-slavery delegates. He refused to move until finished eating; the white delegates present backed him on a vote of the room and then withdrew from the table themselves in protest, which Bowers cited as proof of their sincerity as abolitionists.
attendees: guest ordered from the table; letter-writer

events.name: Weekly Advocate renamed The Colored American; Samuel E. Cornish named Editor
events.description: Philip A. Bell announces that Rev. Samuel E. Cornish -- founder of Freedom's Journal, the first Black-owned American newspaper -- has accepted the post of Editor, and that the paper will be renamed 'The Colored American,' with the change taking effect beginning with the March 4, 1837 issue (Vol. I, No. 9).

events.name: Death of Henrietta Ray, wife of Rev. Charles B. Ray
events.description: Henrietta Ray, wife of the Rev. Charles B. Ray, died in October 1836 aged 22 years 3 months 29 days after a 16-month illness. She had been founding president of a young ladies' literary and scientific society; her death was memorialized by editor Samuel E. Cornish in a sermon extract dated Nov. 13, 1836 and printed March 4, 1837.
attendees: deceased; wife of Rev. Charles B. Ray

events.name: Geneva Colored Anti-Slavery Society formed as NY State Anti-Slavery Society auxiliary
events.description: Formation of the Geneva Colored Anti-Slavery Society, Feb. 17, 1836, as an auxiliary to the New York State Anti-Slavery Society. H. Hawkins served as President and James W. Dulin as Secretary; the society adopted resolutions on abolition, a worldwide concert of prayer, discrimination at the communion table, slaveholding ministers, and support of the abolitionist press. Correspondence forwarded to the Colored American by T. W. Derry of Geneva.
attendees: Geneva correspondent who forwarded the account

events.name: Cleveland, Ohio meeting to petition for repeal of Ohio's Black Laws
events.description: Numerously attended meeting of Cleveland's Black citizens, Jan. 2, 1837, to consider petitioning the Ohio Legislature for repeal of laws oppressing people of color. John Malvin chaired and M. M. Clark served as secretary; Solomon Griffin, H. Hall (of Cincinnati), and John Malvin spoke on the expediency of an agent-driven statewide petition campaign versus a corresponding committee. Resolutions appointed an agent to visit Ohio's towns collecting signatures and a five-person committee (John Malvin, Solomon Griffin, John Brown, Stephen Griffin, M. M. Clark) to direct the agent. At a follow-up meeting Jan. 9, Molliston M. Clark of Philadelphia -- recently a student at Jefferson College, PA -- was appointed the traveling agent.
attendees: appointed traveling agent; of Philadelphia, recently a student at Jefferson College, PA

events.name: Utica fugitive-slave case of "Harry" and companion
events.description: Two Black men, one named Harry, were arrested at Utica on a claim of being fugitive slaves, but escaped. A 'soul-catcher' posing as a Virginia gentleman had tried to bribe Harry into consenting to be sold. The Rev. George Storrs, a Methodist Episcopal minister, met the men afterward and recorded their account; the paper reprints part of his memorandum from a Utica newspaper. The men had in fact left with their deceased master's widow's foreknowledge and consent, so could not legally be claimed as fugitives.
attendees: Methodist Episcopal minister who recorded the men's account

names: City Agent, Clothes Dresser, 27 Thames Street, New York
agents.note: Additional New York City subscription agents for the Weekly Advocate, Feb. 1837.
